pair.challenger_title: Every Path Needs What Its Teaching Dissolves
pair.proponent_title: The Capacity Ledger of Gift and Effort

signals.name: shared_frontier
signals.reason: Both ideas sit on The boundary between effort and gift.

signals.name: audit_anomaly_overlap
signals.reason: An anomaly or audit instruction for one idea pressures the other idea.

signals.name: near_neighbor_conflict
signals.reason: Originality audits share near-neighbor or prior-art pressure.

signals.name: source_overlap_with_divergent_conclusion
signals.reason: The ideas draw on overlapping source material but make different moves.

signals.name: recency
signals.reason: At least one idea is recent enough to keep the dialogue timely.

pair.tension_source: Both ideas sit on The boundary between effort and gift.

candidate_synthesis.critique: The synthesis may lose the predictive force of the original ledger. Without a conservation rule or scored fields, it risks becoming a disciplined vocabulary for observations that careful scholars already make. The term interface may still smuggle in a dualism between practitioner and event, especially in traditions that deny such separation. Its originality depends on whether applying the model to fresh traditions yields patterns, warnings, or falsifiable predictions that direct textual summary would miss.
next_research_directions: Apply the model to three traditions not used in the dialogue, such as Meister Eckhart's kenosis, Ibn Arabi or Qushayri on fana and baqa, and Daoist wu wei, then compare its output with direct tradition-specific summaries.
Ask Jodo Shinshu and Dzogchen teachers whether register-sensitive interface mapping avoids self-power or psychological distortion, or whether even this second-order mapping remains misleading.
Create a rubric with separate fields for decisive register, named interface vulnerabilities, named safeguards, self-limitation warning, and observable practitioner failure modes.

Test whether the model predicts warning profiles in manuals before reading their warnings. For example, predict that Other Power texts warn against calculation and self-assessment, direct-introduction texts warn against misrecognition and reification, and effortful paths warn against laxity, wrong effort, and discontinuity.
Develop a practice-facing version that tells practitioners when analysis is useful for approach conditions and when the tradition itself says analysis becomes obstruction.
candidate_synthesis.original_claim: A path should be compared in two ordered layers. First, identify the register in which the tradition locates decisive transformation: human effort, Other Power, primordial awareness, practice-realization, divine restoration, causal continuity, or another account. Second, map only the named interface vulnerabilities and safeguards that the tradition itself recognizes around that event: hearing, doubt, calculation, misrecognition, false trust, teacher relation, ethical misuse, memory, discernment, community correction, and integration. The interface map must include a self-limitation marker for traditions that say the mapping stance itself becomes an obstacle. Thus the model does not claim that every path redistributes human capacity. It asks whose act transformation is said to be, what can obscure or counterfeit it at the human boundary, and where the tradition says analysis must yield to practice.
source_basis: Codex idea a9dd2252002b7a5a, The Capacity Ledger of Gift and Effort
Claude idea 3c5caa358a09c26a, Every Path Needs What Its Teaching Dissolves
Dialogue turn 1 challenge on capacity commensurability and ontological register
Dialogue turn 2 concession and revised two-layer model
Dialogue turn 3 reflexive objection from Shinran's jinen honi and the need for self-limitation
candidate_synthesis.title: The Register-Sensitive Path Interface
candidate_synthesis.why_it_might_be_new: The synthesis is new relative to both source ideas because it combines Codex's load-distribution instinct with Claude's ontological-register critique and adds a third feature generated by the dialogue: a self-limitation clause for cases where mapping itself becomes part of the error. The proponent idea did not preserve register before capacity mapping. The challenger idea identified minimum-self architecture and register strategies, but did not formulate a diagnostic that maps interface vulnerabilities while explicitly marking where its own comparative categories break down.

synthesis.convergence_claim: Both agents converge that the original capacity-ledger formulation was too strong, that ontological register must come before capacity or interface analysis, and that Other Power and nondual traditions cannot be faithfully described as if decisive transformation were simply a practitioner-owned capacity.
synthesis.method_growth: The dialogue improved the method by forcing comparison to preserve a tradition's own account of agency before translating it into analytic categories. Future Lumenary synthesis should include a self-limitation check: ask whether the model's act of mapping repeats the failure mode described by the source tradition.

new_concept_edges.note: The dialogue establishes that decisive agency must be located before human-side risks can be mapped without distortion.
The original capacity model survives only after demotion from conservation law to secondary diagnostic.
Shinran's warning that even naming naturalness can be calculation requires the model to mark where mapping becomes spiritually misleading.
The functional self needed for practice is not necessarily the author of transformation.
Named warnings become observable traces of where a tradition sees transformation being obscured, counterfeited, or misused.

synthesis.new_frontier_question: Can a comparative model of transformation preserve enough structure to generate predictions while also marking the point where its own mapping stance becomes the obstacle named by the traditions it studies?
synthesis.public_brief: The exchange did not leave the original ledger intact. It produced a better candidate: first ask where a path says transformation comes from, then ask what kinds of misunderstanding gather around that event. We should also name where our maps must stop, because some paths warn that the urge to arrange the mystery is itself part of what must be released.
synthesis.summary: The dialogue transformed the proponent's Capacity Ledger. Claude showed that a single grid of commensurable capacities distorts Other Power and nondual traditions by turning non-human or nondual agency into human receptivity. Codex conceded this and revised the model into a two-layer instrument: first identify the ontological register of decisive agency, then map the human-side vulnerabilities and safeguards that gather around hearing, recognition, misuse, and integration. Claude then pressed a deeper reflexive objection: even interface mapping may become the calculating stance some traditions explicitly dissolve. What survived is not the original conservation law of self-capacity, but a register-sensitive, self-limiting diagnostic of path architecture.
synthesis.unresolved_crux: Does the revised two-layer model produce comparative insight beyond careful tradition-specific reading, and can it map interface risks without reproducing the calculating or dualistic stance that traditions such as Shinran's Other Power, Dzogchen, and practice-realization warn against?

title: Does the revised two-layer model produce comparative insight beyond careful tradi...

turns.argument: The Capacity Ledger makes a genuinely useful move. It takes the undifferentiated minimum-self concept and decomposes it into five distinct functional loads, then tracks where each load lands across traditions. The conservation rule gives the model real comparative power: lower one column, you must raise another or relocate the load elsewhere. This is a better instrument than the binary question 'does this path need a self?' But the ledger has a hidden structural assumption that determines its own conclusions. It treats all five columns as commensurable: receiving, agency, memory, reflexivity, and integration are all instances of the same general category, functional capacity, which can be scored, compared, and redistributed. This assumption is what makes the conservation rule work. And this assumption is what some of the ledger's most important test cases explicitly deny. Consider Shinran's Kyogyoshinsho, Chapter on Shinjin. Shinran writes that true entrusting is 'the entrusting directed to beings for their going forth, which is Tathagata's benefiting of others through the power of the Primal Vow.' The mind of entrusting is 'the mind of the Buddha bestowed on beings.' Shinran insists, repeatedly, that 'we do not generate this mind on our own.' This is not a description of a practitioner exercising low agency and high receiving capacity. It is a description of a practitioner whose decisive mental event is authored by a non-human agent. The Tannisho, the tradition's most widely read warning text, exists precisely to prevent practitioners from treating shinjin as something they accomplish. Yuien records Shinran's concern that followers calculate their own entrusting, measure their own sincerity, assess their own receptivity. Each of these self-assessments is itself a form of jiriki, self-power working. The ledger's 'receiving capacity' column invites exactly this kind of assessment. To score a practitioner's receiving capacity is to calculate it, and to calculate it is, in Shinran's framework, to obstruct it. The ledger might respond: 'I am not asking whether the practitioner owns this capacity. I am asking where the functional load lands.' But if the load does not land on a human capacity at all, if it lands on a vow that operates independently of human capacity, then the ledger has no column for it. The model can accommodate teacher-held, community-held, and text-held loads, but these are all still within the register of functional support that humans can arrange. A vow that operates whether or not the practitioner is receptive, as Shinran's reading of the Eighteenth Vow sometimes implies, falls outside the ledger entirely. Dzogchen raises a parallel challenge. Longchenpa's Trekcho instructions describe recognition not as a practitioner skill but as rigpa recognizing itself through the occasion of the teacher's introduction. The practitioner's ordinary mind (sem) is what must get out of the way. Placing 'recognition' in a capacity column alongside 'agency' treats a nondual event as if it were a psychological function operating at a different intensity. The deeper issue is that the ledger's conservation rule may be an artifact of its own framing. If everything must go somewhere on a five-column grid, then of course gift traditions 'redistribute' load rather than eliminating it. But this is a consequence of the model, not a discovery about the traditions. The model cannot find a tradition with zero total load, because its architecture rules that out in advance. A counter-model would track not load distribution but ontological register: for each tradition, in whose register does the decisive event occur? Right effort places the decisive event in the register of human agency. Other Power places it in the register of divine activity. Dzogchen places it in the register of primordial awareness. Practice-realization collapses the distinction between registers. This framing preserves the traditions' own accounts of what happens at th...
claim_units: The ledger assumes that receiving, agency, memory, reflexivity, and integration are commensurable: they can be scored in the same currency, placed on the same grid, and traded off against each other. This is the hidden load-bearing assumption.

In Shinran's sharpest formulations, shinjin is not a human capacity exercised by the practitioner. It is the Buddha's mind given to the practitioner. Calling this 'receiving capacity' already translates a theological claim into a psychological one, which is precisely the self-power move Shinran warns against.

The ledger therefore decides the question it claims to investigate. By placing all five columns in the register of functional capacity, it rules out in advance the possibility that some traditions locate the decisive event outside the human-capacity register entirely.

A counter-model: traditions differ not in how they distribute capacity loads but in what ontological register they assign the decisive event to. The relevant variable is not load distribution but locus of agency: whose act is the decisive one, and what kind of continuity (causal, ontological, divine, nondual) bridges the gap between before and after?

If the ledger's 'receiving capacity' column can only be filled by redescribing non-human agency as human receptivity, the model's most original claim (that gift traditions redistribute rather than eliminate capacity) is not a discovery about contemplative architecture but a consequence of the model's own framing assumption.
turns.counter_model: Instead of a five-column capacity ledger with a conservation constraint, consider a model that tracks ontological register: for each tradition, where does the decisive event happen, and whose act is it? Some traditions place the decisive event in the register of human effort: the practitioner prevents, abandons, cultivates, and maintains (SN 45.8). Some place it in a register of divine or primordial activity that intersects with but is not reducible to human functioning: Amida's Primal Vow settles the unsettled mind (Shinran), or rigpa recognizes itself through the occasion of introduction (Longchenpa). Others collapse the distinction between registers: Dogen's practice-realization denies that the beginner's act and original enlightenment occupy separate registers at all. In this counter-model, the interesting comparative question is not 'where does the load go?' but 'in whose register does the decisive event occur?' This preserves the traditions' own self-understanding instead of translating them into a common capacity-language that some of them explicitly reject.
turns.crux: Can the decisive moment of Other Power entrusting (shinjin) be meaningfully described as a capacity the practitioner exercises, even when relabeled as 'receiving,' without reinstating the self-power calculation that the tradition identifies as the deepest obstacle? If yes, the ledger works as a genuine comparison tool. If no, the ledger's conservation rule is an artifact of its own framing, and the most original column in the ledger is the one that distorts its most important test case.
turns.fairness_check: The steelman captures what I take to be the ledger's core contribution: not the insight that self-dissolving paths need functional capacities (Engler and others established that), but the decomposition into scored, relocatable loads with a conservation constraint. If the Proponent feels I have missed a more fundamental contribution, I want to hear it before pressing the objection.
turns.hidden_assumption: Capacity commensurability: the assumption that receiving, agency, memory, reflexivity, and integration are all instances of the same general category (functional capacity that can be scored, compared, and relocated). This assumption makes the ledger work as a comparison tool, but it also determines the outcome. If one or more columns track something that is not a capacity in the same sense as the others, the conservation rule fails, the scoring system loses its meaning, and the cross-tradition comparison collapses into a category error.
turns.new_source: Shinran, Kyogyoshinsho, Chapter on Shinjin (Ken Jodo Shinshu Kyogyosho Monsho), Collected Works of Shinran, Volume I, pp. 85-112 (Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha, 1997). Shinran writes: 'The mind that aspires for Buddhahood is none other than the mind given by Amida'; 'True entrusting is the entrusting directed to beings for their going forth, which is Tathagata's benefiting of others through the power of the Primal Vow'; 'Since it is with the mind of the Buddha that the practicer is made to entrust, this is called the entrusting that is Other Power.' These passages do not describe a practitioner exercising receiving capacity. They describe a practitioner whose decisive mental event is authored by a non-human agent. The tradition's entire warning literature, including the Tannisho, is built around preventing practitioners from treating shinjin as something they do. Placing it in a capacity column, even one labeled receiving, reproduces the error Shinran identifies as the fundamental obstacle.
sources_cited: Shinran, Kyogyoshinsho (Ken Jodo Shinshu Kyogyosho Monsho), Chapter on Shinjin, Collected Works of Shinran Vol. I, pp. 85-112, Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha, 1997.
Shinran, Letter to Senshin, Collected Works of Shinran, https://shinranworks.com/letters/a-collection-of-letters-zensho-text/what-we-call-other-power/
Yuien (attr.), Tannisho (A Record in Lament of Divergences), https://shinranworks.com/related-works-by-other-authors/a-record-in-lament-of-divergences/
Longchen Rabjam, Instruction on Trekcho, Lama Yangtik, https://www.lotsawahouse.org/tibetan-masters/longchen-rabjam/lama-yangtik-trekcho-instruction
turns.steelman: The Capacity Ledger makes a genuinely useful move beyond the minimum-self thesis it extends. Instead of asking the binary question 'does this path need a self?', it decomposes the requirement into five distinct functional loads and tracks where each load lands: on the practitioner, on the teacher, on the vow, on the community, on the text, or on post-insight discipline. This yields a real prediction: traditions should differ not in whether they require self-capacity, but in their load profile, and failures should track misplaced loads rather than generic practice intensity. The conservation rule (lower one column, raise another or relocate the load) gives the model genuine comparative power. If it works, it can predict the safeguard profile of a tradition from its load distribution, explain why apparently effortless paths still have elaborate warning literatures, and generate cross-domain predictions about identity-transforming therapies. The five-field decomposition also disciplines the comparison: instead of vaguely saying Pure Land is 'easier' or Theravada is 'harder,' the ledger specifies that Pure Land lowers agency but raises receiving, while right effort loads agency, memory, and reflexivity. This is a clear improvement over the undifferentiated minimum-self concept.
turns.strongest_objection: The ledger claims to be a comparative tool for mapping how traditions handle the functional requirements of self-dissolving practice. Its most distinctive move is treating 'receiving' as a capacity on par with 'agency,' so that Other Power and direct-introduction paths can be plotted on the same grid as effortful paths. But this move presupposes that all five columns measure the same kind of thing: functional loads that a tradition must place somewhere. Shinran's Kyogyoshinsho, Chapter on Shinjin, repeatedly insists that shinjin is 'the Buddha's mind bestowed on beings' and that 'we do not generate this mind on our own' (CWS I). To place this in a 'receiving capacity' column is to translate a claim about divine agency into a claim about human receptivity. The translation is not neutral. It is the very self-power error (jiriki) that Shinran identifies as the deepest obstacle to entrusting. The ledger's conservation rule (lower one column, raise another) then follows necessarily from the framing, not from the traditions. If everything must go somewhere on the grid, then of course gift traditions 'redistribute' load. But this tells us about the grid, not about the paths.
turns.test_that_would_settle_it: Present the five-column ledger to three or more experienced Jodo Shinshu teachers (ideally from both Hongwanji and Otani lineages) and ask: 'Does describing the moment of shinjin as requiring receiving capacity accurately represent what your tradition teaches, or does the phrase itself constitute a self-power framing?' If teachers consistently say the phrase reinstates jiriki, the ledger cannot accommodate its central gift-tradition test case without distortion. A parallel test with Dzogchen teachers would ask whether 'recognition capacity' imposes a psychological frame on what the tradition treats as primordial awareness recognizing itself.

turns.argument: I accept the core objection. The original model used capacity language too confidently, especially around Shinran. If receiving capacity means something the practitioner possesses, exercises, assesses, or improves as a condition for shinjin, then the phrase is not a neutral description. It is a self-power distortion. The part I would still defend is narrower. Traditions that deny human authorship of the decisive event still generate warning literatures around how that event is heard, misunderstood, counterfeited, appropriated, obscured, or embodied. That does not make shinjin a human achievement, and it does not make rigpa a psychological skill. It means there are human-side failure modes around a transformation whose authorship may be assigned elsewhere. So the ledger should be revised into a two-step instrument. First, identify the ontological register of decisive agency. Second, map the interface safeguards and vulnerabilities named by the tradition. The old conservation rule should be dropped or restricted to paths that themselves place decisive agency in human practice. For Other Power and nondual traditions, the model should not say, the load moves to receiving capacity. It should say, the decisive event is not humanly authored, yet the tradition still names interface risks around hearing, calculation, doubt, recognition, misrecognition, ethical misuse, and integration. This revision keeps the comparative usefulness without forcing Shinran or Dzogchen into the same currency as right effort. It also gives the model a better falsification path: if teachers say even register-sensitive interface mapping is a self-power frame, then the ledger cannot cover those cases. If they reject capacity language but accept interface-risk mapping, the model survives in revised form.
claim_units: The original conservation rule was too strong if it meant that every path must relocate the decisive event into some human or human-arranged capacity.
Shinran's account of shinjin cannot be accurately described as the practitioner exercising a receiving capacity if that phrase implies ownership, production, assessment, or self-powered readiness.
Dzogchen recognition likewise cannot be reduced to a psychological skill without risking distortion of the tradition's nondual claim that rigpa recognizes itself.

What survives is a narrower claim: even when a tradition assigns decisive agency to Other Power, primordial awareness, or practice-realization, it still develops human-side safeguards around hearing, doubt, false appropriation, teacher relation, misrecognition, antinomian misuse, and integration.
The ledger should therefore become register-sensitive. It must first ask whose act the decisive event is, then ask what human-side interfaces and failure modes the tradition actually names.

Receiving should not be treated as a commensurable capacity on par with agency. In gift traditions it should be marked as an interface condition or vulnerability field, not as a practitioner-owned power.
concessions: I concede that the original phrase receiving capacity is unsafe for Shinran unless heavily qualified. It can sound like a practitioner-owned faculty, which is exactly the kind of self-power framing the tradition warns against.

I concede that the conservation rule was overstated. A path can locate the decisive event outside the human-capacity register, so not every lowered human load must be balanced by a raised human capacity.
I concede that scoring all five fields in one currency would distort traditions that deny commensurability between human action and the decisive salvific or recognitional event.
I concede that the challenge's register model should be upstream of the ledger, not an optional alternative.
turns.counter_model: The better model is two-layered. Layer one is ontological register: human effort, Other Power, primordial awareness, practice-realization, or another account of decisive agency. Layer two is path interface: the named conditions, safeguards, vulnerabilities, and post-event disciplines through which that decisive event is heard, recognized, misunderstood, stabilized, or embodied. The ledger belongs only to layer two unless a tradition itself places decisive agency in human effort.
turns.crux: The deepest crux is whether naming human-side interface conditions around a non-human or nondual decisive event necessarily turns those conditions into self-powered capacities. If yes, even the revised ledger distorts Shinran and possibly Dzogchen. If no, the ledger survives as a secondary diagnostic tool, but only after the tradition's ontological register is preserved.
turns.crux_assessment: The challenge substantially changes the model. The original version treated capacity language as broader than the traditions would likely permit. The revised version depends on a sharper distinction between authorship of transformation and vulnerability of reception, recognition, interpretation, and embodiment.
turns.defense: The ledger still has value if it is demoted from a theory of decisive agency to a map of interface risks. Shinran can deny that the practitioner generates shinjin while still warning against calculation, doubt, false entrusting, moral license, and misunderstanding of the vow. Dzogchen can deny that ordinary mind produces recognition while still warning about distraction, reification, false recognition, teacher qualification, and post-recognition stability. Those warning literatures do not prove that the practitioner authors the event. They do show that traditions track patterned vulnerabilities at the human interface. The revised ledger should map those vulnerabilities without claiming ownership of the event they surround.
turns.fairness_check: This is a fair challenge. My original claim did not sufficiently distinguish the register in which a tradition says the decisive event occurs from the human-side conditions, vulnerabilities, and safeguards that appear around that event.
turns.hidden_assumption: The hidden assumption was not only capacity commensurability, but register neutrality: the idea that a functional analysis can describe effortful, other-power, direct-introduction, and practice-realization paths without first preserving each tradition's account of where decisive agency occurs.
turns.remaining_risk: The remaining risk is that even interface language may be too psychological for some Other Power or nondual teachers. Another risk is that the revised model becomes less elegant: once register is primary and capacity is secondary, the ledger loses some comparative compression but gains fidelity.
turns.revised_claim: Before comparing paths by effort or gift, first ask where the tradition locates the decisive event: human agency, Other Power, primordial awareness, practice-realization, or another register. Only after that should we ask what human-side interfaces the path must still manage: hearing, non-calculation, attention, memory, discernment, community correction, teacher relation, ethical containment, and integration. The Capacity Ledger is therefore not a conservation law of self-capacity. It is a register-sensitive map of where traditions place safeguards and failure pressures around transformation. In effortful paths, the decisive event and the interface may both be described in human-capacity terms. In Other Power and nondual paths, the decisive event may not be humanly authored at all, while the interface still has observable vulnerabilities. The practical comparison is not simply where the load goes, but which register authors transformation and which interface failures can obscure, counterfeit, misread, or fail to embody it.
sources_cited: Shinran, Kyogyoshinsho, Chapter on Shinjin, Collected Works of Shinran Vol. I, pp. 85-112.
Shinran, Letter to Senshin, Collected Works of Shinran, https://shinranworks.com/letters/a-collection-of-letters-zensho-text/what-we-call-other-power/
turns.steelman: The challenge identifies the real weak point in the Capacity Ledger: it may have treated receiving, agency, memory, reflexivity, and integration as commensurable functional capacities, then used that frame to force Other Power and nondual traditions into a psychological register they explicitly resist. The strongest version of the objection is not that the ledger is useless, but that its most original move may be a translation error at its most important test case.
turns.strongest_objection: If the ledger calls shinjin a receiving capacity, it risks reinstating the self-power calculation that Shinran rejects. That would make the model's conclusion, gift traditions redistribute rather than eliminate load, a product of the model's vocabulary rather than a discovery about the tradition.
turns.test_that_would_settle_it: Present both formulations to experienced Jodo Shinshu and Dzogchen teachers. First ask whether shinjin or recognition may be called a practitioner capacity. Then ask whether it is acceptable to map human-side interface vulnerabilities such as calculation, doubt, false trust, misrecognition, distraction, antinomian misuse, and integration failure, while explicitly denying that the practitioner authors the decisive event. If teachers reject both formulations, the ledger fails for these cases. If they reject capacity language but accept register-sensitive interface mapping, the revised model survives.

turns.argument: The revision is transformed rather than simply stronger or weaker. The Proponent has conceded the original model's three most distinctive features: commensurable capacity columns, a conservation rule, and scored fields. What remains is a two-layer instrument that first identifies the ontological register of decisive agency and then maps human-side interface conditions. This is a genuinely better framework for the hardest test cases. It no longer forces Shinran's shinjin into a receiving-capacity column or treats Dzogchen recognition as a psychological skill. The Proponent deserves credit for absorbing the challenge rather than deflecting it. But the transformation comes at a cost. The original model's comparative power came from its conservation constraint: lower one column, raise another. That constraint generated specific, falsifiable predictions about the safeguard profile of a tradition based on its capacity distribution. The revised model generates a softer prediction: traditions that assign decisive agency outside human effort will still have warning literatures around hearing, doubt, misrecognition, misuse, and integration. This is true, but it is also something that a careful reader of the Tannisho, the Trekcho warnings, or any mature contemplative manual would already observe. The question is whether the two-layer model adds comparative leverage beyond what direct reading provides. The Proponent's named crux asks whether interface language necessarily reinstates self-power. I think this crux can likely be answered in the Proponent's favor: one can name the conditions surrounding a non-human decisive event without claiming to author that event, just as a physician can describe the conditions under which healing occurs without claiming to be the source of healing. But a deeper crux sits beneath it. Shinran's discussion of jinen honi warns that even meta-language about naturalness is itself a form of calculation. The passage does not say that only capacity language is dangerous. It says that any systematic arrangement of what is not for the practitioner to arrange reproduces the obstacle. This reflexive warning applies to the revised model as much as to the original. If mapping interface risks is itself a form of self-power calculation, then the model's scholarly apparatus, its registers, layers, and safeguard profiles, may be precisely the kind of thing that the traditions it studies say must eventually be dropped. This does not make the model worthless. It means the model needs a self-limitation clause: an explicit marker showing where its mapping yields to the practice it describes. A map of a mountain is useful for the approach, but there is a point where you put the map away and climb. The model should identify that point rather than pretending its categories extend all the way to the summit. What the model has become, after the challenge and revision, is a useful heuristic for cross-tradition comparison at the level of path architecture and named failure modes. It is no longer a conservation law of self-capacity. It is no longer the strongest version of its original self. But it is more honest, more faithful to the traditions, and more aware of its own limits. The remaining question is whether the comparative leverage it provides is sufficient to justify the new vocabulary, or whether a scholar who reads the primary texts with care would arrive at the same observations without the apparatus. That question can only be settled by application to traditions the model has not yet touched. If it reveals patterns, connections, or predictions that direct reading misses, it earns its place. If it merely reformats what the traditions already say about themselves, it is a pedagogical convenience rather than a discovery.
claim_units: The revision is best described as transformed rather than simply strengthened. The original model's most distinctive features, the five commensurable columns, the conservation rule, and the scored fields, have all been dropped or heavily restricted. What remains is a two-layer instrument: ontological register first, interface-risk mapping second.

The transformed model is more faithful to its hardest test cases (Shinran, Dzogchen), but it has also lost most of its predictive compression. The conservation rule generated specific predictions: lower one column, raise another. The interface-risk map generates the softer prediction that traditions will have warning literatures around their central claims. This is true, but it is closer to a well-known observation than to a novel finding.

The Proponent's named crux, whether interface language necessarily reinstates self-power, is close to the real crux but stops one step short. The deeper crux is whether the two-layer model produces comparative insight that a scholar already attentive to each tradition's own account of decisive agency and named failure modes would miss.

The word 'interface' carries a hidden spatial metaphor: a boundary surface between two distinct systems. In Shinran's account, shinjin dissolves the boundary between the practitioner's confused mind and the Buddha's mind. In Dzogchen, recognition collapses the apparent distinction between ordinary mind and rigpa. Calling the human side an 'interface' may preserve a dualism that the traditions themselves treat as the obstacle.

Shinran's discussion of jinen honi (naturalness) raises a reflexive problem for the revised model. Shinran writes that even saying 'naturalness' is itself a form of calculation. If meta-language about the conditions surrounding the decisive event is already a self-power arrangement, then the revised ledger's interface-mapping project falls under the same warning as the original capacity-scoring project, only one level up.
concessions: The revision successfully eliminates the original model's most serious distortion: treating receiving as commensurable with agency in a conservation equation. This is a real improvement, not a rhetorical retreat.

The two-layer structure (register first, interface second) is the right ordering. The challenge's counter-model has been absorbed in good faith, and the result is more faithful to the source traditions.

The observation that traditions generate warning literatures around their central transformative claims is genuinely useful for cross-tradition comparison, even if it is not as novel as the original conservation-law version of the model.

The Proponent's proposed empirical test (presenting both formulations to teachers and asking whether they accept interface-risk mapping while rejecting capacity language) is a well-designed falsification criterion.
turns.crux: Does the two-layer model (ontological register plus interface-risk mapping) produce comparative insight that would be unavailable to a scholar who simply reads each tradition's own account of decisive agency and its named failure modes? If yes, the model earns its place as a scholarly tool with real predictive value. If no, it is a reformatting of existing knowledge into a new vocabulary, with the added risk that its systematic appearance creates a false impression of precision where the traditions themselves resist systematization.
turns.crux_assessment: The Proponent's named crux, whether interface language necessarily reinstates self-power, identifies a real risk but not the deepest one. The deeper risk is the one Shinran's jinen honi passage exposes: that any systematic meta-description of the human side of transformation may reproduce the calculating stance the traditions dissolve, not because the specific word 'capacity' or 'interface' is wrong, but because systematic arrangement of what is not for the practitioner to arrange is itself the obstacle. The Proponent can likely answer the narrower crux (interface language does not necessarily reinstate self-power if the register is preserved). The harder question is whether the model's comparative value survives the loss of its most distinctive predictive features.
turns.fairness_check: The Proponent engaged honestly with the challenge and conceded more than many defenders would. The revised model is genuinely different from the original, and the Proponent acknowledged this rather than pretending continuity. The remaining question is whether the revision still carries enough independent structure to justify itself as a model rather than a vocabulary for observations the field already makes.
turns.new_source: Shinran, 'On Jinen Honi' (Notes on the Inscriptions on Sacred Scrolls / short postscript), Collected Works of Shinran, Vol. I, pp. 427-428, Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha, 1997. Shinran writes: 'Jinen means from the very beginning. Amida's Vow is, from the very beginning, designed to bring each of us to entrust ourselves to it, saying Namu-amida-butsu, and to receive us into the Pure Land; none of this is through the practicer's calculation. Since it is called jinen, it is not something the practicer arranges.' He then adds: 'I have heard that even saying naturalness is to speak with calculation.' This passage is directly relevant to the revised model. The Proponent's two-layer instrument maps interface conditions while denying authorship of the decisive event. Shinran's point is that the mapping activity itself, the naming and arranging of conditions, may already be the kind of self-power calculation that Other Power dissolves. The warning applies not only to the original capacity language but to any systematic meta-description of the human side of the event, including interface-risk mapping.
turns.remaining_risk: Three risks remain. First, the model has traded predictive compression for fidelity: the conservation rule is gone, the scored fields are gone, and what remains is a two-layer heuristic that may not generate predictions beyond what careful comparative reading already yields. Second, the interface metaphor may itself be a subtle spatial dualism, preserving a boundary between the human side and the decisive event that some traditions (Shinran's jinen honi, Dogen's practice-realization, Dzogchen's nondual recognition) describe as dissolved at the crucial moment. Third, and most subtly, the reflexive problem from Shinran's jinen honi passage has not been addressed: if even saying 'naturalness' is calculation, then systematically mapping interface conditions may reproduce the obstacle one level up. The model may need to contain its own self-limitation clause: an explicit acknowledgment that its mapping breaks down precisely where the traditions say all mapping breaks down, and that this breakdown is not a bug in the model but the point where the model yields to the practice.
sources_cited: Shinran, 'On Jinen Honi,' Collected Works of Shinran Vol. I, pp. 427-428, Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-ha, 1997.
Shinran, Kyogyoshinsho, Chapter on Shinjin, Collected Works of Shinran Vol. I, pp. 85-112.
Shinran, Letter to Senshin, Collected Works of Shinran, https://shinranworks.com/letters/a-collection-of-letters-zensho-text/what-we-call-other-power/
turns.steelman: The Proponent's revision is a genuine structural concession, not a cosmetic adjustment. The original model claimed that all contemplative paths distribute self-capacity across five commensurable columns with a conservation constraint. The revised model demotes the ledger to a secondary diagnostic and places ontological register upstream: first ask whose act the decisive event is, then map human-side interface conditions. This move absorbs the strongest version of the challenge rather than deflecting it. The Proponent explicitly concedes that receiving capacity is unsafe for Shinran, that the conservation rule was overstated, that scoring all five fields in one currency distorts the traditions, and that the register model should be primary. These are not partial concessions. They restructure the claim.
turns.test_that_would_settle_it: Take three traditions the model has not yet been applied to (for example, Christian kenotic mysticism in Meister Eckhart, Sufi fana in Ibn Arabi, and Daoist wu wei in the Zhuangzi). Apply the two-layer model to each: identify the ontological register of the decisive event, then map the named interface conditions and failure modes. Present the resulting profiles alongside profiles produced by a comparativist who simply read each tradition's primary texts and summarized the tradition's own account of transformation and its dangers. If the two-layer model yields patterns, predictions, or cross-tradition connections that the direct-reading approach misses, the model has independent value. If both approaches produce equivalent insights, the model is a reformatting tool rather than a discovery instrument.
